data.title: Omron Corporation: A Moaty, Diversified Industrial Firm Positioned For Secular Growth
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome AR Insights as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Introduction

The market displays excessive negativity toward Omron Corporation (OTCPK:OMRNY) because its stock performance has substantially trailed the Nikkei 225 during the past five years. Omron maintains its leadership position in industrial automation and healthcare devices and infrastructure systems despite current revenue challenges. The company benefits from persistent market trends related to labor shortages and digital healthcare and energy sustainability, which create opportunities for sustained growth. According to my assessment the company presents an opportunity to purchase shares at a discount of 31% against its actual worth.

Company Overview

As a global leader in industrial automation and healthcare technology and social infrastructure systems, Omron Corporation operates within the Electrical Equipment industry. The Japanese-based company operates from its headquarters in Kyoto and maintains 28,450 employees spread across the world as of March 2024. Through its diverse business operations Omron provides automation systems alongside health monitoring devices as well as electronic components and data-driven services. Omron Corporation operates as a Tokyo Stock Exchange (TSE: 6645) listed company and trades under the ticker symbol OMRNY in the United States through American Depositary Receipts (ADR). The company operates through five distinct business segments that make up its overall structure.

Industrial Automation (IAB) serves as the company’s largest division which generated 42.7% of net sales while producing 67.1% of operating profit. IAB delivers factory automation equipment consisting of programmable logic controllers (PLCs) sensors and motion control devices and vision systems and collaborative robots. Automotive manufacturers together with food and beverage producers and semiconductor companies utilize its products in their operations.

Social Systems, Solutions and Service (SSB) represents 18.5% of net sales while operating profit from this segment equals 31.1%. The SSB division operates infrastructure systems across Japan to deliver automated fare collection systems together with ticket vending machines and traffic control systems and solar power monitoring systems. Omron Corporation became the first company to develop an automated ticket gate system when it launched this technology in 1967.

Healthcare (HCB) generates 17.1% of net sales and delivers 32.4% of operating profit. Omron Healthcare produces digital blood pressure monitors together with thermometers and ECG devices and nebulizers and body composition analyzers. Omron Healthcare stands as the worldwide leader for digital blood pressure monitor production.

The Devices & Module Solutions (DMB) division generates 16.7% of total sales while producing 0.6% of the company's operating profit. DMB operates as an electronic component manufacturing business which produces relays and switches and connectors and MEMS sensors. These products serve multiple sectors including automotive systems together with consumer electronics and industrial equipment and mobile devices.

Data Solution Business (DSB) operates as the newest segment which generates 5.0% of net sales and 5.2% of operating profit. The DSB segment delivers data-driven services mainly through digital healthcare operations. The segment operates as a result of combining JMDC operations with medical data analysis and remote diagnostic services and insurance-related health data services.

Eliminations and Others: The segment shows intra-company adjustments together with corporate expenses which result in a 36.3% reduction of consolidated operating profit. Business units within the company eliminate internal transactions through this segment and corporate expenses remain unallocated to specific segments. Executive compensation together with centralized research and development expenses as well as information technology infrastructure and corporate office operational costs fall under this category.

The revenue breakdown of Omron shows that Japan represents its largest market with 44.3% followed by Greater China at 20.0% and Europe at 14.9% and the Americas at 10.6% and Southeast Asia and Others at 10.0%. The company gets 0.3% of its total revenue through direct exports which involve Japan-to-overseas customer sales without regional subsidiary involvement. I believe that Omron operates with sufficient geographic diversity throughout its operations.

The Tokyo-listed shares of Omron Corporation (6645.T) have performed poorly versus the Nikkei 225 during the last five years, decreasing by about 50% while the index rose by 70%. The stock has dropped to JPY 3,800 which remains close to its 52-week minimum of JPY 3,600 and significantly below its 52-week maximum of about JPY 6,800. The current stock price stands at JPY 3,800 while market sentiment appears overly negative. This combination of long-term underperformance with a low share price indicates a potential value opportunity for investors. The stock has good potential for a market revaluation when Omron's fundamentals show improvement against the broader Japanese market.

Industry Analysis

Industrial Automation & Factory Control

The global industrial automation & control systems market was valued at approximately US $206 billion in 2024 and is forecast to reach US $379 billion by 2030, growing at a CAGR of ~10.8%. The market expansion stems from increasing interest in AI predictive maintenance solutions together with IoT integration and robotic systems, especially in Asia-Pacific which made up 39% of the market during 2024.

Omron operates in the market through its IAB segment which delivers controllers, sensors, relays, vision systems, and motion control equipment. Omron occupies a position in the top PLC vendors alongside Siemens, Rockwell and ABB with approximately 8% market share.

Threat of New Entrants — Low

The establishment of industrial automation operations demands substantial financial resources for research development alongside manufacturing infrastructure and sales distribution networks. New businesses must overcome extensive product development timelines alongside certification processes and develop industrial client trust before entering this market. The industrial automation sector functions under control of established giants Siemens AG (OTCPK:SIEGY), ABB Ltd. (OTCPK:ABBNY), Rockwell Automation (ROK), Mitsubishi Corporation (OTCPK:MSBHF) together with Omron. Most new startups face a long and difficult process to establish themselves as major market players.

Supplier Power — Moderate

Specialized components' suppliers including semiconductors, sensors and precision parts have some ability to influence prices, especially during global supply chain issues. Large companies such as Omron benefit from better supplier agreements because their global operations and extensive scale enable them to secure advantageous purchasing terms.

Buyer Power – Moderate

Large industrial clients possess some negotiating power regarding prices yet Omron's equipment becomes difficult to replace due to its integrated nature and customized design and high deployment costs. The power of buyers becomes restricted after the integration process particularly in long-life automation environments.

Threat of Substitutes — Moderate

Substitutes include alternative automation approaches such as software-only solutions, cloud-based controls, or emerging AI-driven platforms reducing hardware dependency. While software and cloud solutions grow, the replacement of full hardware systems remains limited because physical sensors and PLCs and motion controllers are fundamental for operation.

Competitive Rivalry: Moderate

The market stability and product specialization alongside limited major competitors and high customer costs for switching result in reduced market competition. The rivalry operates at a moderate intensity which does not reach the intense competition found in fragmented markets such as food service or fashion retail.

Home Healthcare Devices

The global home healthcare devices market reached a value of US $178.5 billion in 2024 and is projected to grow to US $417 billion by 2032 at a Compound Annual Growth Rate (OTC:CAGR) of 11.2%. The market growth results from three key factors including an aging population and the increasing number of chronic diseases alongside the rising adoption of home monitoring solutions. North America maintains its position as the largest market but Asia-Pacific shows the fastest growth rate because healthcare services are becoming more accessible and consumers are becoming more health-conscious.

Omron dominates this sector through its Healthcare Business (HCB) segment that offers a range of FDA- and CE-cleared medical devices including blood pressure monitors, digital thermometers, electrotherapy devices (TENS units), nebulizers, body composition monitors and portable ECG devices. The company leads the market as the leading manufacturer of digital blood pressure monitors. Omron is also expanding its ecosystem with the Omron Connect app and cloud platform, integrating device data for health tracking and early intervention as part of its vision of a “Going for Zero” society—aimed at eliminating strokes and heart attacks through preventive monitoring.

Threat of New Entrants — Moderate

The entry barrier for medical-grade devices exists because manufacturers need to meet FDA and CE requirements as well as perform clinical validations. Basic health products such as thermometers and fitness trackers can be entered by new businesses at a lower regulatory threshold. The brand trust of Omron alongside its regulatory knowledge and worldwide distribution channels creates market advantages but the entry of new competitors with minimal validation requirements poses price competition threats to lower-end segments.

2. Supplier Power — Low

Omron sources commonly available components like sensors, semiconductors, and casings. The combination of Omron’s large procurement volume and its worldwide supplier network decreases the long-term influence of suppliers even though global chip shortages might create short-term difficulties.

3. Buyer Power — High

In the home healthcare device industry, buyers — primarily individual consumers — hold substantial power. Most devices are widely available, easy to replace and share standard features generating intense market competition, especially in the low- to mid-price product segment.

The market has price-sensitive customers who shift between different brands or models by checking reviews and checking prices on the internet or retail locations. The market contains many generic products along with original equipment manufacturer brands that push established companies to maintain aggressive pricing. Omron and other trusted brands benefit from consumer confidence but still are not immune to the effects of high buyer leverage in this competitive and transparent marketplace.

4. Threat of Substitutes — Moderate

Smartwatches (e.g., Apple Watch, Galaxy Watch, Fitbit) now feature ECG, blood oxygen, and heart rate variability tracking, blurring the line between consumer tech and medical monitoring. The majority of existing devices do not achieve complete medical-grade standards and fail to replace diagnostic equipment when used for regulated medical applications. That said, continued tech innovation poses an increasing threat to single-purpose medical devices, especially if future updates receive regulatory approval.

Competitive Rivalry — High

The home healthcare device market is crowded and fragmented, especially in categories like blood pressure monitors and thermometers. Numerous manufacturers—particularly from China and India—compete on price, availability, and basic functionality, intensifying rivalry in the value segment.

In the premium segment, competition is shaped by firms offering clinically validated, accurate, and reliable devices. Established players such as Omron, Microlife, and Welch Allyn (BAX) compete based on brand trust, clinical certification, and increasingly on integration with remote monitoring services. Nonetheless, Omron is likely to thrive regardless of the price competition brought on by these companies as it has garnered the trust of doctors and pharmacists — an important and sticky characteristic. It remains the #1 recommended brand for blood pressure monitors, for example.

Improving Profitability Despite Decline in Revenue

Omron’s revenue has been growing at 3.4% CAGR for the past 5 years. However, due to the recent slowdown in investment in semiconductor and EV industries and tariffs, Omron’s revenue suffered a consecutive decline of 6.8% and 2.1% in the last two fiscal years.

In response, Omron’s management has shifted their focus from top-line growth to bottom line profitability. As a result, despite a second sale decline of 2.1% in FY 2024, its operating income has rebounded by 55.9%. Based on Omron’s competitive advantages, I believe Omron would certainly outlive this recent downturn (occasional cyclical downturn is inevitable in manufacturing sectors), and strong secular trends discussed below would drive long-term growth in Omron’s earnings.

Limited Debt & Steady Dividend

Omron demonstrates strong financial discipline and a consistent commitment to return value to shareholders.

The company maintained zero debt on its balance sheet for nearly a decade until FY2023. Even after taking on debt in FY2023 and FY2024, Omron maintained a healthy balance sheet, with cash comfortably exceeding total debt in both years.

Over the past ten years, Omron has steadily increased its annual dividend from 68 JPY per share to 104 JPY per share, while maintaining a consistent level of R&D expense (ranging from 5.5% to 6.8% of sales, average at 6.3%) to maintain its competitive position in its industry areas where consistent innovation and product improvement is indispensable.

Economic Moat & Secular Tailwinds

Industrial Automation

Omron’s industrial automation segment provides a broad range of factory automation equipment including programmable logic controller (PLC), sensors, and robots. This business area’s moat stems from its switching cost and reputation.

Omron’s automation equipment is deeply integrated into manufacturers’ systems. Switching to a different supplier usually requires reprogramming software systems and retraining staff, and would increase the risk of malfunctioning. Consequently, customers tend to remain with their current supplier, contributing to sticky revenue for Omron.

Moreover, manufacturers usually require equipment with high precision and durability, so they tend to choose from providers with a long track record of supplying reliable equipment. This forms a barrier to entry into the field, enabling suppliers like Omron with decades of experience to maintain their market share.

Despite recent headwinds leading to consecutive declines in revenue for the last two fiscal years, I believe this segment would experience long-term growth due to two secular trends. First, most developed countries are experiencing the problems of an aging population and increasing cost of labour (for instance, Japan’s over‑65 demographic reached a record 29.3% in 2024), hence the need for factory automation would be even greater in the future. Second, as the Internet of Things (IOT) becomes increasingly prevalent in the manufacturing sector, Omron’s smart devices that enable collection of real-time data from machines and connection to cloud/database would experience an increase in demand.

Healthcare

The 2025 investor presentation of Omron shows that blood pressure monitors (BPMs) generate 64% of healthcare segment sales with nebulizers accounting for 15%. The company leads the global market with 45% market share of home-use BPMs and 25% market share of nebulizers.

Omron has a track record of consistent innovation in home healthcare. It introduced the first home-use blood pressure monitor in 1973 and launched the world’s first wearable BPM in 2019. In 2024, Omron received FDA De Novo clearance for its IntelliSense AFib algorithm, which is the first AI-based atrial fibrillation detection tool integrated into a home-use blood pressure monitor.

The trend toward remote diagnosis and telemedicine continues to accelerate. The global remote patient monitoring (RPM) market is projected to grow at a CAGR of 18.6%, reaching USD 16.9 billion by 2030. Omron maintains a dominant market position and continued investment in developing new products which positions it well to capture the long-term growth of connected home healthcare solutions.

Social Infrastructure Systems

The majority of revenue in this segment comes from energy solutions (e.g. storage batteries and power conditioners) (42%) and railway services (e.g. passenger gates and ticket vending machines) (24%). Omron developed the world's first automated ticket gates and ticket vending machines in 1967 and has maintained a leading market share of approximately 50% in these products in Japan.

Railway operators prioritize reliability, as equipment malfunctions during operating hours can impact passenger traffic. Consequently, they tend to use equipment vendors with a long proven track record like Omron, as switching suppliers could introduce risks.

Omron’s social infrastructure products are often integrated into broader systems of their customers, making switching suppliers costly and complex. The integration between Omron products and their customers leads to customer loyalty and customer retention.

The main driver of expansion in this segment has been energy solutions which showed a 20% CAGR during the past five years. The low penetration rate of energy storage systems and power conditioners in homes, coupled with global trends toward sustainability, should serve as secular tailwinds for Omron’s energy products.

Comparable Comps Analysis

Compared to other large-cap diversified industrial peers, Omron appears attractively priced across EV/Revenue, EV/EBITDA, and P/Tangible Book Value, trading below the peer average on each of these metrics. The only exception is its P/E ratio, which currently appears elevated at 46.4x.

The high P/E ratio does not reflect the actual core earnings power of the company. The FY2024 results were impacted by a JPY 27.8 billion one-time restructuring charge that resulted from the reduction of approximately 2,000 employees. Omron's normalized FY2024 net income would amount to JPY 44.1 billion after removing this exceptional expense which would result in a P/E ratio of 18x and align with peer companies.

DCF Model

My calculation of intrinsic equity value for Omron amounts to JPY 1,093 billion (USD 7.58 billion) but its current market capitalization stands at JPY 756 billion (USD 5.24 billion) resulting in an undervaluation of about 31%.

To account for cyclicality in Omron’s core Industrial Automation business, I adjusted operating income by applying the average operating margin over the past 10 years (8.62%) to the company’s trailing twelve-month (TTM) revenue of JPY 801.8 billion. This approach aligns valuation with Omron’s current operational scale while mitigating the impact of margin fluctuations driven by manufacturing sector downcycles.

The 3.4% growth rate to perpetuity reflects the company's five-year historical revenue CAGR of 3.4% which I believe will be a reasonable long-term estimate given , supported by structural drivers in healthcare, automation, and energy systems.

I used a cost of capital of 5.5% because Omron’s risk profile is low given its diversified revenue streams, leadership in specific markets and solid balance sheet.

The formula I used to calculate the intrinsic value is

Where growth rate divided by return on capital is used to estimate the reinvestment required to produce the growth anticipated, which is therefore subtracted from adjusted operating income to obtain free cash flow. Compare this to standard cashflow formula:

Risks

Omron’s Industrial Automation segment, which has accounted for approximately half of the company’s total revenue over the past decade, remains highly sensitive to the cyclical nature of global manufacturing activity. The demand for factory automation solutions will decrease if manufacturing sectors experience slow down and reduction in their capital spending.

Omron Healthcare continues to dominate the global market for blood pressure monitors and nebulizers but faces growing competition from other companies. The expansion of low-cost personal healthcare devices in the market makes it difficult for the company to keep prices up and differentiate its products. High-end smart health devices are increasingly dominated by technology giants like Apple (AAPL), Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) and Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACY) because of their strong research capabilities and complete digital systems which create a major competitive threat.

In China, Omron’s position in the industrial automation market is under pressure from both rising domestic competitors and industrial policies favoring local production. These challenges include price-based competition and government initiatives — such as “Made in China 2025” — that prioritize the development and adoption of domestically manufactured automation equipment.

End Note

I recommend a Buy on Omron Corporation, with a fair value estimate of JPY 1,093 billion (USD 7.58 billion) —representing a 31% upside from its current market capitalization. The combination of Omron's strong financial position, its disciplined approach to capital distribution and its long-term structural growth opportunities provides the basis for a valuation re-rating as the cyclical pressures subside.

data.title: Without GLP-1 Hims & Hers Is A $20s Per Share Stock -- Sell Call
data.description:
Hims & Hers (NYSE:HIMS) stock plunged almost 35% on Monday to $41.98 per share after Novo Nordisk (NVO) announced that it would be ending its collaboration with the telehealth company that put the blockbuster weight loss therapy, Wegovy, on Hims’ platform.

Hims is still up 104% YTD, priced into that is the run-up on the compounded weight loss sales allowed while original treatment was in shortage, the sell-off after original treatment was removed from the shortage list, the positives gained in late April when news of the Novo partnership broke, the loss of that partnership earlier this week, and the latest commentary from Hims' CEO Andrew Dudum reassuring investors and patients that the company would continue to "offer cheaper, compounded versions of GLP-1 weight-loss drugs." The chart below shows the trajectory of events.

Hims' stock was down roughly 50% between its February highs of $68.74 and April 29th, the day the partnership was announced, since which it surged 83% prior to Monday's crash. There is a lot of PTSD when it comes to buying the dip on Hims because of its crash in late February, which dragged the stock price down to around $23 per share. Hims is a pretty volatile stock, with more than 94 moves over 5% over the past year, and even investors' positive reaction to Dudum's reassurance this week, with the stock trading over 19% on Thursday, confirms it. We think investors should stay away for now because while the negative of losing the deal is priced into the stock, it's not priced into 1. the outlook, or 2. the average revenue per subscriber.

We also have little visibility on how Hims will continue to meet patient requirements for weight-loss drugs without the Novo partnership. Even if we do assume the worst is priced in, we don't see any near-term tailwinds to drive upside into the next quarter. We recommend investors wait until the stock visits the EMA200 level of $36 per share before thinking about jumping back in.

Part of why Monday’s sell-off was so intense is because the breakup wasn’t just a deal termination; it also was full of drama, which we cannot get into here due to the accusatory nature of the claims thrown around. However, we think it's safe to say that Novo and Hims are unlikely to team up anytime soon. In fact, Novo has already courted another company, WeightWatchers, to spur access to Wegovy. This not only confirms that Novo is already moving on, but it also makes the market a lot more competitive for Hims, considering that starting July 1st, WeightWatchers customers can access Wegovy through CenterWell pharmacy with introductory pricing of $299 and $199 for those getting a refill.

The Novo partnership was crucial for Hims to continue its growth trajectory, with its outperformance largely reliant on its ability to tap into the legal and affordable weight-loss treatment. Keep in mind that on the Q1 earnings call, Dudum stressed:

Teaming up with Novo Nordisk is a pivotal milestone.

Now that the partnership is off the table, all the positives it was supposed to bring to Hims' financials are too.

Timing of earnings matters, a lot

If we look at Hims’ financials, the company last reported in May for the quarter ended March 31st, and guided FY25 sales in the range of $2.3B to $2.4B versus consensus for $2.32B and for Q2 sales of $530M-$550M versus estimates for $564.57M. The stock took a hit on the report, although subscribers grew 38% to 2.4M and sales were up 111% to $586M. The quarter didn’t price in the Novo deal positives, but the outlook, reported after the deal announcement, could very well be priced in. The deal was a big topic of conversation on the call. The chart below outlines the company’s trajectory of sales, operating margin, and subscribers.

When it comes to the near-term, we do think that there is a higher risk heading into the next print because 1.) Outlook could have priced in some of the Novo attraction to customers, and more importantly, 2.) We don’t know what Hims’ financials will look like without its stronghold on GLP-1.

Remember, the stock surge in February was because of its benefit from the weight loss compound being on the shortage list, and that’s why it drove the stock. Then, even in Q1, the numbers reported reflected the benefit of the ramped sale of cheaper copies, since the quarter ended in March, and the FDA prohibited the sales of copies as of May 22nd.

The metrics we care about at the end of the day are two: subscriber growth and monthly online revenue per average subscriber. While subscriber growth is steadily on the rise with data indicating increased traffic on the site, average revenue per user wasn’t until all the weight-loss drama entered the conversation. In Q1, that number hit $84, up 52.7% Y/Y from $73 the quarter prior in Q4, and $67 in Q3, and prior to that was constrained within the $50s range without a material uptick, as shown below.

This makes us more concerned about the trajectory of average revenue per subscriber, considering it was only carried higher on the weight loss drugs, specifically the cheaper copies.

What to do with the stock?

Hims has taken a nosedive that has de-risked it from losing the partnership with Novo; however, the risk now is on worsening financials without having a solid foot in the weight loss treatment market. We see some more downside in the near term before it's time to revisit the stock when it’s below $40 per share, edging closer to its EMA200 support of $36.20.

Hims isn't going anywhere, and we do think this player will make a comeback in the mid to long term due to the fact that Hims is much bigger than GLP-1 (Glucagon-like peptide-1), which is the compounded semaglutide used in weight loss medications, Ozempic and Wegovy. The company is exploring new ventures into menopause and testosterone, which are piled on top of Hims’ treatments in: weight loss, mental health, hers derm, hims derm, and sexual health. These two could be massive markets to tap into, with the menopause treatment market estimated to grow at a CAGR of 4.8% and testosterone at 4%, respectively. This makes us more positive on the Hims in the mid-to-long term, but we do think the market needs to reset expectations for the stock and its access to weight-loss drug tailwinds in the near term.

data.title: Glencore: Supportive Release On Coal And EVR, Buy Confirmed
data.description:
In our previous review, we highlighted Glencore's (OTCPK:GLCNF) (OTCPK:GLNCY) relative underperformance versus peers and suggested a timely tactical entry – an approach that has since proven effective (Fig. 1). Our stance on the company's coal exposure has evolved. While we had previously factored in valuation pressure stemming from Glencore's coal investments, today, we see support for a new strategy. The core EBITDA remained supported by the Marketing segment and copper operations. With supportive free cash flow yields, we continue to see a compelling risk-reward profile.

Fig. 1

Q1 Production Report And Coal Asset Overview

Glencore's first quarter production update (with no financials), released at the end of April, fell short of company-compiled consensus expectations. However, the recent announcement of a revised coal strategy represents a constructive shift and offers clear support to the investment case.

Very briefly, Glencore's copper production was weak. In numbers, the output was 30% lower than last year (Fig. 2). This was due to water availability at the Collahuasi mine. That said, this should be the toughest quarter of the year. The Kamoto site had an underground mine issue and reduced labor availability. On a positive note, both zinc and coking coal were 4% and 7% up vs. expectations. Cobalt production reached 9.5kt, but DRC announced a four-month ban on cobalt sales in February 2025. Production volumes are expected to be more heavily weighted toward the year's second half, reflecting the typical seasonality of Glencore's operational profile.

On the guidance, the Glencore management team indicated that the 2025 marketing core operating profit would be in the middle of the $2.2–3.2 billion ranges and guided energy coal production at 87–95Mt (down from 92–100Mt - Fig. 3). This represents a 5% reduction from the announcement to reduce Cerrejón volumes. Still, on a positive note, the company expects stronger copper output thanks to Collahuasi's improvement, with the completion of the planned reorientation of the pit and lower strip ratio in Peru.

Source: Glencore Q1 production report - Fig. 2

Fig. 3

Despite a softer Q1 and revised guidance, we see upside potential.

  1. Glencore ranks among the largest coal exporters, in addition to its diversified base metals portfolio (copper, zinc, nickel, and cobalt). The decline in coal prices has weighed on the share price, but we believe that thermal and coking coal prices are approaching – or may have already reached – levels near marginal cost support (around $100/t for thermal and $175/t for coking). We acknowledge the risk of further downside, particularly given elevated Chinese stockpiles and seasonally weaker demand during the shoulder periods. Having said that, the company hosted a site visit to its EVR, a mine acquired from Teck in 2024. We now have numbers that set out a medium-term coal strategy. Glencore coal EBITDA is expected to reach $1.5 billion in H1 due to the lower prices, but given the thermal coal forward curve, we see potential for at least a $5 billion EBITDA (Fig. 5). EVR is a top-quartile mine with 30+ years of reserve life (Fig. 4). On a negative note, still related to the met coal, the company estimates that the current price of 90mt is cash-negative for most of the mines and expects more closures to be announced. That said, the industry now has a healthier balance sheet, so this could take longer than previous cycles.
  2. Steel production is likely growing and is driven by Indonesia, India, Malaysia, and Vietnam.
  3. All other 2025 production outlooks were unchanged, besides energy coal (Fig. 3). In our numbers, we now assume zero cobalt sales until Q3, which also strips $400 million from our copper EBITDA. In our model, considering a positive contribution from the Marketing division, our EBITDA reached $5.5 billion with a 2025 EBITDA outlook of $12.5 billion. Glencore's Marketing division has often been viewed as a 'low-hanging fruit' for criticism, partly due to historical controversies and regulatory scrutiny. That said, we believe its platform is a key differentiator, particularly in coal. With dedicated teams across 21 countries – covering both major producing and consuming regions – the company gathers on-the-ground intelligence on supply dynamics, pricing, quality variations, and customer needs. This information is centralized at the Zug headquarters, enabling market insight and responsiveness that few peers can match.
  4. On the shareholders' upside, we see the potential for Glencore to monetize the Bunge shares and announce a new $1 billion share repurchase.

Source: Group coal asset overview - Fig. 4

Fig. 5

Valuation

Q1 production was somewhat disappointing; however, the company's recent sell-side analyst visit to EVR and its accompanying presentation highlighted signs of a recovery cycle in the coal segment. This emerging momentum is the key upside that allows us to value the business on a normalized EBITDA basis. Based on a new normalized figure, we are maintaining a 3x multiple on the coal assets, reducing the aggregate EBITDA to $5 billion from $5.3 billion, so our sub-segment valuation arrives at $15 billion. Continuing to value Glencore metals division with an EV/EBITDA of 8x, our valuation reached $77.6 billion. This EV/EBITDA target is below comps such as FCX and SCCO. So, our total enterprise value reached $92.6 billion. Our normalized Glencore EBITDA is $14.7 billion, while this year's forecast is around $12.5 billion. With a lower EBITDA projection, we lower our FCF estimates and increase the company's financial obligations (from $31 billion to $31.9 billion). With the latest changes, Glencore's equity value (EV) reached $60.7 billion. With an upside of more than 25%, we set a buy rating, with a target price of £3.7 per share ($10.16 for the GLNCY ADR).

Risks

Our analysis includes a comprehensive view of Glencore's downside risks. Key concerns range from a potential slowdown in global economic activity and further downward revisions to GDP forecasts to declining commodity prices, particularly cobalt and copper. The company reports Q2 production on 30 July and H1 results on 6 August. We see a risk that the 2025 copper guidance will be at the low end of its range. This is due to the weak production in Q1. We also highlight the risk of value-destructive M&A, growing ESG-related pressure, and increasing regulatory scrutiny. Commodity and currency volatility remain structural challenges. In addition, the industry is vulnerable to political, financial, and operational shocks, while faster-than-anticipated production increases from new mines may further weigh on pricing dynamics.

Conclusion

We view the valuation as attractive relative to peers, with free cash flow yield around 8–9% at what appear to be cyclically depressed price levels. We also see catalysts in the ongoing buyback and potential restructuring initiatives. While the downside risk in coal prices is limited, the medium-term outlook for copper and zinc remains compelling. Near-term free cash flow and cash returns are resilient even in a downside scenario. For this reason, we confirm our buy rating.

data.title: Shopify Vs. PayPal: Circle's USDC Momentum Is A Gamechanger For Shopify At The Expense Of PayPal
data.description:
Stablecoins have been regaining traction as of late as a result of the combined effect of Circle’s (NYSE:CRCL) blockbuster IPO, regulatory progress, and meaningful industry partnerships supportive of use cases that can bolster longer-term adoption.

Specifically, Circle – the issuer of the world’s second-largest stablecoin, USD Coin (USDC-USD) – has recently gone public to strong investor demand. Its shares have gained close to eight-fold since its IPO before paring some gains this week. Momentum for stablecoins has also picked up in response to the U.S. Senate’s recent endorsement of the GENIUS Act for further review and approval by the House of Representatives and President Trump. Progress on this front remains key to improving regulatory clarity over the use of stablecoins, facilitating further development of use cases to bolster longer-term adoption.

Against this backdrop, Shopify (NASDAQ:SHOP) has emerged as a surprising early winner. Despite its limited direct participation in the issuance and wallet storage of stablecoins, Shopify recently partnered with Coinbase (COIN) to integrate the latter’s Base protocol. The partnership would effectively allow Shopify to facilitate USDC payments across its global merchant network, positioning it at the forefront of stablecoin adoption in commerce settings. Based on recent disclosures from the CEOs of Coinbase and Shopify, this marks the first time “a large-scale e-commerce platform [is] adopting crypto payments”, effectively improving payment efficiency through faster transaction times and lower fees. This is expected to create a favourable economic loop for all of Shopify, its merchants, and end-market consumers as stablecoin usage – particularly, USDC – scales.

Meanwhile, PayPal (PYPL) appears to trail the emerging tailwinds facing Shopify in the trajectory of stablecoin adoption. Although the payment solution provider launched its own stablecoin in 2023, PayPal USD (PYUSD-USD), which is currently supported by Coinbase Commerce, it lacks the same reach and momentum displayed by USDC. PayPal also doesn’t yet offer payment solutions for its merchants to accept stablecoin payments beyond PYUSD at checkout, which further limits its participation in the evolving stablecoin economy compared to Shopify.

Given Shopify’s stronger alignment to the leading USDC, and its much broader merchant network to propel the adoption flywheel, the company is not only positioned to capture a greater share of early stablecoin-driven commerce gains. It’s also slowly, but surely, outpacing PayPal in shaping the next phase of digital payments, which represents an additive opportunity to its longer-term financial trajectory.

What are Stablecoins?

Stablecoins represent a category of cryptocurrency that’s often pegged to the value of a benchmark asset, such as the U.S. dollar, to ensure stability of value versus other crypto assets like Bitcoin (BTC-USD). Since cryptocurrencies are typically subject to intense volatility, stablecoins are designed as an alternative to minimize exposure to price fluctuations, making them more attractive and applicable for everyday transactions in commerce, particularly cross-border payments. For instance, if Bitcoins were used at checkout for an item priced at 1 BTC, and the item needs to be refunded within a seven-day return window, then it’s likely the value of the 1 BTC at the time of refund has already changed significantly from what it was at the time of the initial transaction’s completion due to the cryptocurrency’s inherent volatility. Yet, stablecoins aim to eradicate that risk, which has been a key roadblock in crypto adoption for commerce use cases.

USDC is currently one of the world’s most widely adopted stablecoins after Tether (USDT). It’s pegged 1:1 to the U.S. dollar, with each token fully backed by cash or short-term U.S. Treasury. Following its issuer Circle’s recent IPO, USDC has gained incremental mainstream visibility and institutional confidence. This has been further bolstered by recent regulatory progress pertaining to the use of stablecoins, making USDC an attractive alternative for use within the regulated U.S. market. And USDC’s growing role in commerce use cases is evident in its recent adoption by Shopify. This was made possible following the leading e-commerce host’s recent partnership with Coinbase and Stripe in creating and implementing the open source “Commerce Payments Protocol” based on alignment with improved regulatory clarity outlined under the GENIUS Act.

In addition to USDC, PayPal also issued its proprietary stablecoin PYUSD in partnership with Paxos Trust Company, which is pegged 1:1 to the U.S. dollar as well. While PYUSD is supported by Coinbase Commerce, which can be implemented by any merchant to support stablecoin checkout, its usage remains largely restricted through the PayPal Checkout network. As a result, the level of scale, liquidity, and merchant adoption observed for PYUSD continues to trail that of USDC by wide margins.

What is the Base Protocol?

As mentioned in the earlier section, Shopify has recently joined hands with Coinbase and Stripe in developing the open-source Commerce Payments Protocol. This framework is based on Coinbase’s proprietary Base protocol, which is a commerce payments infrastructure developed on Coinbase’s Layer 2 blockchain, Base. The protocol is designed to ensure the use of stablecoin in payment transactions can unlock greater time and cost efficiencies under a secure environment on a chain through six key functions:

  • Payment authorization – initiates and provides immediate feedback on approval or decline of the transaction; if the transaction is approved, then the buyer’s USDC consideration is immediately taken into an escrow account. The USDC leaves the escrow account only when the merchant captures them by confirming delivery of the service and/or shipment of the good, a refund is issued, or a void transaction is triggered on the chain.
  • Void – facilitates cancellation of authorized payment before it’s finalized, whereby USDC held in escrow are returned to the buyer. This usually happens when an issue occurs, such as insufficient funds in the buyer’s wallet to complete the transaction.
  • Reclaim – reverses uncollected or unfulfilled transactions, whereby authorized USDC held in escrow are returned to the buyer.
  • Capture – immediately after the transaction is authorized, USDC leaves the buyer’s wallet but is held in escrow; it only leaves escrow when the merchant “captures” the payment by signaling acceptance of the transaction.
  • Refund – if a refund is requested and approved within the refund window set by the merchant, then the funds captured are automatically returned to the buyer on the chain via the protocol.
  • Final – marks payment completion with the USDC funds fully in the merchant’s possession; refunds are no longer an option on the chain once this stage is reached.

It’s evident the end-to-end infrastructure support by the Base protocol through the broader payment cycle mirrors closely to that of card payment rails. However, it promises significantly lower fees and faster settlement times by eliminating intermediary layers often required in facilitating traditional card transactions. As a result, the Base protocol enables frictionless, borderless commerce, which aims to benefit all participants – spanning the operator (Coinbase), transaction processing partner (Stripe), platform host (Shopify), merchants, and consumers – on the chain.

Shopify - A Key Beneficiary of Stablecoin Commerce

As mentioned in the earlier section, Shopify’s direct participation in stablecoin developments has largely been absent to date. Yet through its recent partnership with Coinbase to implement the Base protocol and facilitate USDC payments across its platform, Shopify is poised to gain a first-mover advantage and emerge as a key beneficiary of potential mass market adoption of stablecoin use in commerce. The company has already started rolling out USDC payment support for certain merchants in early access, with broader deployment across its network to materialize over the coming year.

The implementation of USDC payments at checkout in Shop Pay and Shopify Payments merchants is expected to represent an additive opportunity for Shopify’s operating unit economics. Currently, Shopify charges merchants about 2.4% to 2.9% on card purchases swiped through the Shopify Payments network. Meanwhile, the charge for USDC payments is comparatively lower, with early adopting merchants “in the U.S. and other select countries” subject to eligibility for a rebate of up to 0.5% on related transactions.

Although fees charged on stablecoin transactions are typically lower than those observed on traditional card transactions, the net take-rate receivable by Shopify and merchants actually end up much higher. This is because USDC transactions, for instance, aren’t exposed to the incremental interchange, network, and web-hosting fees often observed in traditional card rails. Specifically, USDC transactions processed via the Base protocol are currently only subject to processing partner fees (1.5%) to Stripe, protocol operator fees (1%) to Coinbase, and other ancillary administrative costs such as consumer cash-back rebates (1%) to Shopify. This accordingly results in superior net take rates on stablecoin transactions beneficial for both Shopify and its merchants, even if the gross fees collected are slightly lower than those observed for traditional card transactions.

Adopting the Base protocol to facilitate USDC payments is also expected to expand the broader commerce addressable market for Shopify, especially given that stablecoin is currently the world’s second-largest holding within its asset class amongst users. And Shopify’s support for multi-wallet USDC payments offered to consumers, and zero FX or currency conversion fees charged offered to merchants only further strengthens the case – especially in cross-border transactions. This represents a key competitive advantage for Shopify in incentivizing adoption, especially given how critical the incremental cost-savings are for merchants amid rising macro headwinds and the impending threat of reciprocal tariffs.

Shopify’s extensive reach into global commerce also represents another avenue for which it can become a key beneficiary of emerging stablecoin adoption in payments. Although the company no longer discloses the size of its merchant base, industry estimates currently put the figure at more than four million. With its global merchant base currently facilitating a quarterly GMV run-rate of about $75 billion based on the Q1 earnings disclosures, Shopify’s scale represents a key gateway in accelerating USDC integration across everyday commerce. Coupled with maturing stablecoin regulation with the U.S. Senate’s recent endorsement for the Genius Act, Shopify stands to become a key beneficiary in making crypto-native payment rails a staple in real-world transaction flows at scale.

Taken together, Shopify’s facilitation of USDC payments is poised to unlock an additive revenue stream to further support its longer-term growth trajectory, while also improving its net take-rate unit economics. More importantly, we see an opportunity for Shopify to benefit from a positive flywheel capable of driving sustained accretive upside through its facilitation of stablecoin usage over the longer term. With expectations for the latest development to accelerate mainstream USDC adoption in commerce, Shopify is poised to benefit from the ensuing economic gains to its earnings outlook. In turn, the additional resources unlocked are expected to help further scale stablecoin payment facilitation beyond USDC across Shopify’s global commerce network over the longer term. This is likely to reinforce stablecoin adoption in commerce, which will further solidify Shopify’s reach into related opportunities and ensuing unit economic benefits over time.

PayPal - Trailing in the Stablecoin Commerce Opportunity

Although PayPal has been integrating stablecoin-driven commerce capabilities within its ecosystem since its launch of PYUSD in 2023, the company has yet to demonstrate material participation in related transactions. This shortfall is expected to become especially prevalent following Shopify’s recent integration of the Base protocol to facilitate USDC payments across its global commerce network.

As mentioned earlier, PYUSD is currently supported by Coinbase Commerce for integration across Coinbase’s merchant partners. It’s also supported by PayPal Checkout, giving PYUSD exposure to the company’s very own merchant base. Yet, adoption across both platforms remains largely nominal compared to USDC, which is in the process of being integrated across Shopify’s merchant base of more than four million. As a result, this is expected to further diminish PYUSD’s appeal and widen its distance from USDC in merchant integration and consumer familiarity, representing a marked challenge for PayPal’s foray into stablecoin commerce opportunities.

Admittedly, PYUSD can be conveniently converted to USDC, since it’s also a Dollar-pegged stablecoin, which technically allows PayPal’s alternative to ride on the coattails of impending momentum in USDC adoption. However, the process entails added friction at checkout across Shopify’s extensive USDC-supported merchant base, nonetheless, which is expected to significantly deter PayPal’s participation in mostly impulsive purchases. Specifically, recent industry estimates predict that up to 80% of e-commerce transactions are a result of impulse purchases, highlighting the potential of lost participation for PYUSD in global commerce without direct support at checkout.

PayPal’s limited participation in stablecoin commerce is further limited by its current support for PYUSD at checkout only. Recall from the earlier discussion that the only stablecoin PayPal accepts within its payment solutions ecosystem is PYUSD. Given PYUSD’s scale is significantly smaller than USDC’s, this narrow approach inadvertently represents an inherent restriction to PayPal’s participation in stablecoin commerce flows.

Taken together, PayPal’s benefit from the emergence of stablecoin-driven commerce remains comparatively indirect and delayed against Shopify’s approach underpinned by the direct facilitation of USDC payments. Specifically, limited merchant and consumer use of PYUSD and PayPal’s inherently restricted exposure to stablecoin commerce within its network today represent an inherent curtailment to its participation in the new payment rail’s promised unit economic improvements. And this challenge is expected to further, considering the intensifying competition posed by Shopify’s ongoing rollout of USDC payment facilitation at scale across its global merchant base. Coupled with the recent improvement in market sentiment for stablecoin usage, backed by regulatory support, PayPal’s backfoot positioning in related opportunities could represent a near-term headwind that shouldn’t be dismissed.

Final Thoughts

The accelerating momentum in USDC adoption, reinforced by Circle’s successful IPO, regulatory progress, and Shopify’s implementation across its global commerce system, marks a potential inflection in stablecoin-driven commerce. We view Shopify’s staged rollout of USDC support at checkout as an emerging tailwind that shouldn’t be dismissed, as it underpins its potential to become the most immediate beneficiary in stablecoin commerce.

By integrating the Base protocol and facilitating USDC at checkout across its global merchant network, Shopify is poised to gain a first-mover advantage in participating in the improved unit economics promised by the stablecoin payment rail. Not only is this expected to improve Shopify’s take-rate profile, but the facilitation of USDC payments is expected to represent a scalable, additive revenue stream to its longer-term growth profile. More importantly, every USDC transaction facilitated through the Shopify network is expected to amplify the adoption of stablecoin commerce, cementing the company’s role as a key enabler of the technology.

In contrast, PayPal’s comparatively modest and restricted approach in supporting stablecoin commerce observed to date risks ceding ground at a critical inflection point in payment solutions. The absence of broader stablecoin payment solutions beyond PYUSD in PayPal’s network, and inherently limited use of PYUSD amongst merchants and consumers, represent a bottleneck for the company for now. This accordingly puts PayPal at a disadvantage compared to Shopify, especially in an environment where merchants and consumers are increasingly seeking cost efficiencies to mitigate impending cyclical headwinds facing commerce.

Taken together, USDC’s momentum represents more than just a breakthrough for Shopify. It also marks an emerging shift in digital commerce and payment solutions infrastructure that PayPal has yet to prove it can materially capitalize on. As Shopify deepens its role in driving USDC adoption in global commerce across its network, it stands to benefit disproportionately from the structure efficiency and network effects that follow.
